scenario: A FEEL expression must safely check whether a variable customer exists (is not null) before accessing customer.name.
question: Which FEEL approach fits?

options.text: name else "Unknown" uses an invented function name. The actual FEEL existence-test built-in is is defined(customer) not exists(). While the if-then-else structure is correct, using the wrong function name exists() would cause the condition to evaluate incorrectly or produce a runtime error. 0 in Camunda 8.

name is optional chaining syntax from Kotlin, JavaScript (ES2020), and Swift. FEEL does not support the ?. optional chaining operator. Using this in a FEEL expression produces a parse error because the syntax is not valid FEEL grammar. Understanding this characteristic is essential for the Camunda 8 Certified Professional examination. This pattern is covered in the official Camunda 8 documentation.

name else "Unknown" uses FEEL's if-then-else expression with a direct null comparison. This is valid FEEL syntax and safely guards against null access. name, "Unknown") per FEEL boolean functions returns the default when the first argument evaluates to null. Both are valid FEEL null-safety patterns. Teams building on Camunda 8 should consider this when designing process models for production deployment.

The claim that FEEL automatically handles null property access without any explicit guard is risky. Accessing a property on a null context in FEEL typically returns null but can behave unexpectedly depending on the expression context. Explicit null checks via if-then-else or get or else() are the recommended safe patterns for production FEEL expressions. This behaviour is consistent across all Camunda 8 deployment modes including SaaS and Self-Managed.

a.text: Incorrect. if customer != null then ... else ... works. Cleaner FEEL-native one-liner: get or else(customer.name, "Unknown") per feel-built-in-functions-boolean.md:70-91.

b.text: Incorrect. No exists(). Real FEEL idiom is is defined(customer) per feel-built-in-functions-boolean.md:29-59.

c.text: Correct. Optional chaining is Kotlin/JS/Swift; not in FEEL.

d.text: Incorrect. FEEL on null-property-access typically returns null but it's better to explicitly check.

explanation: Optional chaining is Kotlin/JS/Swift; not in FEEL.
